Inscription: In Memory Of Professor Robert D. Brackett National Director And Founder Of Sigma Rho Tau 1966

Title: St. Andrew’s Episcopal Church
Inscription: St. Andrew’s Episcopal Church had been organized by the spring of 1828. It incorporated under an act of the Territory of Michigan in 1833. One year later the congregation bought land on this site from newspaper editor George Corselius for $105. The first church, a small wooden structure, was consecrated in 1838 and enlarged in 1856. St. Andrew’s hired architect Gordon W. Lloyd to design this Gothic Revival church. His design was built in stages: the sanctuary was completed in 1869; the chapel and a rectory called Tatlock Hall, in 1881; and the church tower, in 1903. Tiles made by Pewabic Pottery were added to the interior floor in 1914. The cloister was added in 1932. St. Andrew’s is Ann Arbor’s oldest church building in continuous use and has earned state and local preservation awards. The church helped establish nearby Episcopal churches and founded the Shelter Association of Washtenaw County in 1982 to house homeless individuals.
Erected by: Michigan Historical Commission - Michigan History Center

Title: St. John's Evangelical Lutheran Church
Inscription: In 1869 German settlers, led by the Reverend Frederick Schmid, organized the first Lutheran congregation in Northfield Township. The original church on the site was dedicated in 1874. On March 13, 1932, the church and parsonage were destroyed by fire. The present church and parsonage were dedicated that year.

Title: St. Patrick's Church
Inscription: Catholicism in Northfield Township dates from the early nineteenth century. In 1829 Father Patrick O’Kelly, a native of Kilkenny, Ireland, was sent to the area to minister to the Irish Catholics who were settling in southwestern Michigan. The first parish church, a log structure, was completed on this site in 1831. Originally named St. Brigid, this is the oldest English-speaking Catholic parish in the state. The present Gothic Revival-style church was completed and dedicated in 1878. The parish was renamed St. Patrick’s at that time. The rectory was completed in 1890. In 1917 the church and rectory were badly damaged by a cyclone; however, both were rebuilt in subsequent years. Serving the area for 150 years, the parish continues to reach out to the needs of the surrounding community.
Erected by: Michigan History Division, Department of State. (Marker Number S494.)

Title: St. Patrick's Civil War Memorial
Inscription: Civil War Veterans 1861 - 1865 William W. Boyle Daniel Donovan Patrick Leanord James W. Mead Maurice O'Conner Patrick H. O'Connor James Sage John Shannon James W. Tobin Michael Trainor James Welch John O'Connor James Kain

Title: St. Thomas the Apostle Church
Inscription: In 1831 Father Patrick O'Kelly came from Detroit to minister to the Irish Catholics in the Washtenaw area. He offered his first mass in Ann Arbor on July 12, 1835, in a home located on land bounded by Detroit, Kingsley and Fifth Streets. St. Thomas parish took more permanent form in 1840, when under the leadership of Father Thomas Cullen it purchased land on Kingsley Street near Division Street and built Ann Arbor's first brick church. It established a parochial school in 1868. The church site was purchased in 1883. The present church, begun in 1896 under Father Edward D. Kelly, was dedicated in 1899. Its granite field stone and Bailey bluestone were donated and hauled to the site by parishioners and area farmers. The first hospital to serve the community and two additional Ann Arbor Catholic parishes emerged from St. Thomas parish.
Erected by: Bureau of History, Michigan Department of State. (Marker Number L1181.)

Title: Samuel Denton
Inscription: Professor of Medicine in the University of Michigan for ten years from 1850. He taught Pathology and the Theory and Practice of Medicine. Born July 2, 1803, at Vallkill, New York, he studied medicine at Castleton, Vermont where he received the M.D. degree in 1825 and came to Ann Arbor. He was elected to the state senate and the overnor appointed him Regent of the University, an office he held for four years. He died on August 17, 1860. With the calm faith of a Christian, he departed into the peace of the dead.

Title: Schools and Churches
Inscription:
Ann Arbor public school, begun in the 1830s, were inadequately funded and poorly organized. This changed in 1856 when the Union School opened (building on right); it was the grandest building in Ann Arbor. Its assembly room, seating 700, was often used for public events. Classes prepared students to enter the nearby university. Nonresidents, who paid tuition, made up more than half the enrollment. 

Religious and private schools also were available. In the nineteenth century most of Ann Arbor's churches were nearby. They established reading rooms, social centers, and lectures to attract university students. The 1882 First Unitarian Church (across State Street to your left) had a social hall and reading room. Directly behind you, the Episcopalians built Harris Hall in 1886 for students to meet "under the refining and elevating influence of the Church's social life." A later pastor said their goal was "to intercept students on their way to Joe's and the Orient," two Main Street saloons famous in Michigan student tradition where "the minds and habits of the young should not be formed." Nearer campus on State Street, the Student Christian Association built Newberry Hall (later the Kelsey Museum) in 1891 and then Lane Hall in 1915 for student social and religious activities.

Title: Schools In Lower Town
Inscription:
Early Settlers in Lower Town had large families. Good schools were a part of their New England expectations. Both private and public schools were maintained at several temporary Lower Town locations.
In the 1830s the Misses Azuba and Elizabeth Vail taught private classes in the basement of their residence at the end of Maiden Lane at what would now be 1030 Broadway. In 1838 Baptist Rev. Thomas Holmes taught pupils in a private school in the small church meeting house on Wall Street. The first public schoolhouse in Lower Town was erected about 1837 on Maiden Lane.
By late 1841 Rev. Holmes taught at a second public school at 1202 Traver-the oldest school building still Standing in Ann Arbor. Abolitionists Josiah Beckley and William Perry were two of its early directors. The two school districts combined in 1855 to become the Union School on Wall Street. Union became the Fifth Ward School when Lower Town finally became a part of the city in 1861
School teaching was no easy job in the 1890s. D Miss Anna Clinton, who taught for forty-seven years, was twenty when she started teaching at the Fifth Ward School.
She got the first salaried teaching position by virtue of the fact that her predecessor was knocked down by one of the husky fellows in the first grade and refused to teach again at that school.
Miss Clinton described the Fifth Ward School as being entered from Wall Street through a vestibule, which served as cloakroom for thetwo lower rooms. In one corner stood the bucket and dipper from which everyone drank. On either side of the vestibule were tow large classrooms. A handsome staircase led up to two more classrooms. Six grades were housed in the school.
Miss clinton's room had a small amount of storage space which held ink and paper, supplied by the school, and craft material, supplied by the teacher. The room was heated by a large stove that frequently went out.
There was no basement under Miss clinton's room-only a crawl space filled with green scum-covered water in summer and iced over in winter. Antoher unappealing feature of the school was its proximity to the slaughterhouses on the river. Miss Clinton said that twice during the fall the slaughterhouse flies migrated to the school in such numbers that it was necessary to close school and exterminate them. Those were the days when Canal Street behind the school was known as "Shinbone "Alley."
Christmas was celebrated by the children and their parents in the frame one-story Baptist church across A spring exhibition was held in the classroom and the year's projects were displayed. Miss Clinton praised the fine cooperative spirit of the Fifth Ward parents during all the years in which she worked with them.
The Donovan School, although the smallest school building in the district, had a large attendance. It had no auditorium and no real gymnasium, and the principal's office was crowded into a cloakroom. To compensate, a strong teacher-student-parent cooperation was established that resulted in a close relationship between learning and doing.
Since The Donovan School could not foster an adequate athletic program, it developed other means of holding the loyalty and affection of the children. This was accomplished through plays and public performances (known throughout the city for their quality), the Donovan News, group projects, parent-teacher programs, and famed circuses staged for several successive springs on Mrs. Elizabethe Leaird Slack's lawn on Broadway.

Title: A Second Shopping District
Inscription:
Town and gown came together on State Street after 1870 to create Ann Arbor's second shopping district. Grocers, barbers, and tailors served both communities, while billiard parlors, dining halls, bathhouses, and bookstores catered to the growing student population. John Nickels Meat Market, added to the front of his home, was one of the early commercial buildings transforming the neighborhood. After 1890 the streetcar linked Main Street and campus, and several downtown businesses added branches here. 

J. J. Quarry opened a drugstore in his new building at State and North University in 1898. It specialized in supplies for nearby university hospitals and the medical and dental schools, as well as the offices of doctors and dentists over neighboring shops. 

James Foster's House of Art, which opened in 1906 featuring Arts and Crafts gifts, drew an affluent and sophisticated clientele to State Street. In 1915 Tom Nickels replaced his father's meat market with the city's first shopping arcade. The terra-cotta building's bank, luxury shops, and post office branch symbolized State Street's twentieth-century identity as a fashionable shopping destination. That market was challenged in the 1960s by shopping malls, the lifting of UM's ban on cars for undergraduates, and changing tastes of the Vietnam War era. By the end of the century, chain stores, coffeehouses, restaurants, and sportswear shops replaced many older businesses. Books and music remained staples: Tome and Louis Borders opened their first bookstore here in 1971.

Title: Shagbark Hickory
Inscription:
Mighty Tough 
• The National Forest Service's manual of native trees states that "no commercial species of wood is equal to it in combined strength, toughness, hardness, and stiffness." 

• The wood is excellent for burning and is used to flavor foods like hickory-smoked bacon. 

• Look for three other shagbark hickories growing nearby: across the adjacent path and along the Huron River. 


A Gift of Cherry Trees 
Also in 1952, U-M President Harlan H. Hatcher received a gift of 182 flowering cherry trees. 

The Japanese ambassador Eikichi Araki presented the trees to the University of Michigan on behalf of the people of Japan and U-M alumni living in Tokyo. This cherry tree is one of very few that survive from over a half century ago.
Erected by: Matthaei Botanical Gardens and Nichols Arboretum, University of Michigan

Title: Shopping on East Huron Street
Inscription:
Advertising signs and cards display the wide variety of goods offered in the 1870s by the businesses pictured above. The plank sidewalk, gas lanterns, and dirt road have long since been replaced by concrete, bright lights, and blacktop. All the buildings east to Fourth Ave., including the imposing Cook House Hotel, are gone. 

But in the block beyond, some remain behind facades that reflect changing needs and styles over time. The brick building on the corner of East Huron St. and South Fourth Ave. where Caspar Rinsey sold groceries has long been hidden behind painted aluminum siding.

Title: Simpson Memorial Institute
Inscription: The Thomas Henry Simpson Memorial Institute for Medical Research combined medical research and patient care activities, provided research experience for medical and postgraduate students, direct care to its patients on the third floor, and consultant services to patients at the University Hospital. Among its contributions were the development of a new agent for the treatment of pernicious anemia, the isolation of vitamin B-12, improved understanding of the use of blood and blood substitutes in the treatment of shock, and insight into the causes and treatments of leukemia and other neoplastic diseases of the blood. A gift to the University by Catherine MacDonald Simpson as a memorial to her husband, a Detroit industrialist who died of pernicious anemia, the building, designed by Albert Kahn, now serves the Department of Internal Medicine and the Historical Center for the Health Sciences in their research activities. Medical School Sesqucentennial 2000

Title: Social and Political Change on South University
Inscription: When local merchants began the Ann Arbor Art Fair in July 1960, South University catered to both townspeople and students. During 40 years of social and political change, the fair grew into a city-wide extravaganza. In the twentieth century, as fraternities, sororities, dormitories, and student rooms concentrated nearby, South University had become a focus of student activity. At this corner in the 1950s and 1960s you could have seen homecoming parades or panty raiders shouting "To the hill!" (women's dorms), in the 1980s a basketball riot, and the 1990s the Naked Mile. Political activities as well as pranks have always been a part of student life. Earlier students, fueled by alcohol, youth, and boredom, had torn up the town's wooden sidewalks for bonfires, disabled trolley cars, and shouted down presidential candidate William Jennings Bryan. They vigorously debated abolition, temperance, wars, and women's suffrage. During the Vietnam War, Ann Arbor became a center of the nationwide social and political firestorm. Students for a Democratic Society (SDS) and peace teach-ins originated at UM. Those turbulent years began with civil rights picketing, intensified with antiwar protests, White Panthers, Black Action Movement strikes, and demonstrations for women's liberation and gay rights. "Make love not war!"and "Power to the people!" affronted middle-class values and expressed the new rebellious spirit that led to hippies, the sexual revolution, and the Hash Bash.
Erected by: Ann Arbor Historic District Commission and South University Area Association

Title: South University and Forest Avenues
Inscription: Blaich's grocery, added to an old house on this site in 1895, was the first commercial building on South University. Forty years later, as Miller's, it was a popular place to enjoy ice cream. sodas, and sandwiches. The old wooden building was demolished in 1964 to make way for University Towers. Miller's reopened here in the new building. Centicore, a bookstore identified with the rebellious spirit of the sixties, opened next to it. Miller's closed in 1985-like the Food Mart down the street, a victim of rapidly rising rents that would force out many other local businesses. The Campus Theater was replaced by a mini-mall and the street's last gas station by a two-story commercial building. In 2006 new zoning encouraging high-density commercial and residential development was adopted to reinvigorate South University. Within months, the city approved a ten story apartment building to replace the historic 1923 Anberay Apartments on East University.

Title: Spanish-American War Memorial
Inscription: Erected In Honor Of The U of M Men Who Fought In The War Against Spain Class of ’99

Title: The Staeblers and the Germania/American Hotel
Inscription: In 1885 Michael Staebler, a successful farmer from a pioneer German family west of town, constructed this building as the Germania Hotel. Balls, lectures, musical programs, and meetings of the Germania Society were held on the third floor. Staebler later added a fourth story and renamed the building the American Hotel. He set his sons up in business. Albert managed the hotel and Edward ran a bicycle shop and coal business. The large box shown above in front of the store displayed different grades of coal. A third son, Fred, ran a grocery store across the street. Next door to the hotel, Heinzman and Son offered cash to local farmers for hides and furs.

Title: The Streets Around Courthouse Square
Inscription: For over one hundred years the streets surrounding Courthouse Square were a focus for busy Ann Arbor life. Most structures that housed the many businesses and activities around the square are gone. No photo exists of Miss Monroe's primary schoolhouse, a crude log building with small glass windows and split log benches. It stood behind you on the northwest corner of Main and Ann streets from 1825 to 1829, at a time when the square served as town founder John Allen's vegetable patch and members of the Potawatomi tribe traded berries in town. Mary Foster, Ann Arbor's first female lawyer, graduated from the University of Michigan in 1876 and ranked high among the profession.

Title: A Supply Side of Town
Inscription: By the 1880s the area between downtown and the railroad along the Huron River supplied lumber, carts, carriages, stone work, coal, oil, and gas. Agricultural Hall, built in 1856, served multiple industrial, civic, and commercial functions before neighborhood industry declined by the 1920s.

Title: Sutherland–Wilson Farm
Inscription: Established by Langford and Lydia Sutherland in 1832, the farm operated for more than 150 years, producing crops and livestock. Most of the land was later sold for development, while the remaining acreage became a preserved historic farm museum.

Title: Tappan Hall
Inscription: Completed in 1894 and named after university president Henry Philip Tappan, this building has housed multiple academic departments and remains the oldest extant classroom building on the University of Michigan campus.
Erected by: University of Michigan History and Traditions Committee

Title: Terhune Memorial Park
Inscription: Terhune Memorial Park preserves gravestones moved in the 1920s from an earlier cemetery. It commemorates Revolutionary War patriot Ensign John Terhune, his wife Sarah Vreeland Terhune, and pioneer child Emily Whitmore, reflecting early American patriotism, migration to Michigan Territory, and local memory practices.
Date erected: Unknown (1920s relocation; marker date not specified)

Title: Thomas Church / Saint Thomas Cemetery
Inscription: Founded by German immigrants associated with the Basel Mission Institute, Thomas Church and Saint Thomas Cemetery illustrate early German settlement in the Ann Arbor area. The church and cemetery, established in 1844, preserve German-language worship traditions and distinctive nineteenth-century funerary art.
Erected by: Michigan Historical Center, Michigan Department of State

Title: Three Generations of Metzgers on Washington Street
Inscription: This marker documents the Metzger family’s German restaurants, founded in 1928, which served Ann Arbor residents across generations. The businesses reflect immigrant entrepreneurship, cultural adaptation during World War II, and the role of family-run restaurants in the city’s social life.

Title: Ticknor-Campbell House
Inscription: In 1844 Dr. Benjamin Ticknor built this Classic Revival cobblestone house on his Pittsfield Township farm, with an older 1835 frame wing first occupied by his brother Heman. After Ticknor’s death in 1858, his medical and classical library went to the University of Michigan. In 1881 educator William Campbell bought the property; farming continued here until 1955. The City of Ann Arbor purchased the house in 1972 as a pioneer farm museum, and it was later placed on national and Michigan historic registers.
Erected by: Michigan History Division, Department of State

Title: Town and Gown: Entertainment and Changing Culture
Inscription: Ann Arbor’s cultural identity grew from “town and gown” entertainment traditions, from 19th‑century performances in ballrooms, churches, and halls to purpose-built venues such as Hill’s Opera House (1871) and University Hall’s auditorium (cornerstone laid 1871). The Michigan Theater (opened 1928) and Lydia Mendelssohn Theatre (1929) expanded shared cultural life, while organizations like the Ann Arbor Symphony, Civic Theater, and UM groups sustained the tradition through the 20th century; the 1954 Michigras parade symbolizes this ongoing civic–university connection.

Title: Transportation, Electric Power, and Gas
Inscription: Lower Town’s late‑19th‑century prosperity was shaped by mills, factories, and rail connections across the Huron River, including trestles rebuilt after failures and disasters. In the early 1900s, the closure of the Agricultural Works, the 1904 Argo Mills fire, and rail accidents marked instability, followed by industrial reorganization and infrastructure upgrades. The district then shifted toward electric power generation along the river as Edison interests consolidated water rights and built hydro dams (beginning with Barton Dam in 1912), helping supply electricity for the region and streetcar/interurban systems, before later university-driven redevelopment needs emerged.

Title: The Underground Railroad
Inscription: Rev. Guy Beckley served as a “conductor” on the Underground Railroad, using his home near Pontiac Trail as a secret station; freedom seeker Caroline Quarlls stayed with him en route to Canada. Ann Arbor’s anti-slavery organizing included the founding of the Michigan Anti‑Slavery Society in 1836 and publication of its newspaper, The Signal of Liberty, starting in 1841 from the Huron Block across Broadway. The marker also highlights long-standing African American community presence and advocacy, including delegates to the 1843 state convention of colored citizens and the integration of Ann Arbor schools.

Title: The University Expands
Inscription: Beginning in the early 20th century, the university’s northward expansion replaced a neighborhood of large homes with major cultural facilities. Hill Auditorium (1913), donated by Regent Arthur Hill and designed by Albert Kahn, anchored a growing arts and performance district, followed by landmarks such as the Michigan League (1929), Burton Tower (1936), Rackham (1938), and the Power Center (1971), funded largely through private giving. The marker emphasizes how fundraising networks—especially women students and alumnae—helped build key institutional spaces and how coeducation contributed to campus growth.

Title: University of Michigan Campus 1907
Inscription: By the end of President James B. Angell’s long tenure, prominent buildings lined State Street, reflecting the university’s rapid growth and changing campus image. The marker references key facilities and designs from this period—law, museums, memorial halls, libraries, hospitals, and scientific infrastructure—while noting that only a small subset of Angell-era buildings remained by the late 20th/early 21st century. Together the images frame campus development as an evolving institutional landscape shaped by expansion, replacement, and preservation.

Title: The University of Michigan Men's Glee Club
Inscription: Founded in 1859, the University of Michigan Men’s Glee Club is described as the oldest student‑run organization at UM and the second‑oldest collegiate glee club in the United States. The plaque, placed near Hill Auditorium (a long‑time home venue), commemorates 150 years of musical tradition, camaraderie, and choral achievement.

Title: Upper Level of the Barn
Inscription: The marker explains how the barn’s upper level functioned within the farm’s spatial organization: the upper portion stored straw and hay, supported threshing of oats/wheat, and held key equipment, with areas likely used for grain storage. It also describes the adjacent Barn Yard system of animal shelters (hog house, sheep shed, stables/chicken coop, lower barn for cows/cattle) and the fence, gate, and shared water trough connecting Farm Yard and Barn Yard circulation.

Title: Uptown Theaters
Inscription: Tracing Ann Arbor’s movie‑theater corridor, the marker highlights the Michigan Theater’s opening on January 5, 1928 (silent film plus live orchestra and organ), the 1929 conversion to sound films, and Depression‑era mixed programming. It notes the State Theater’s 1942 opening, mid‑century consolidation as Main Street theaters closed, and later threats from television and suburban multiplexes. Community fundraising saved and restored the Michigan after its 1979 closure, transforming it into a nonprofit venue for independent film, festivals, concerts, and live performance.

Title: Village of Dixboro
Inscription: Founded in 1824 by Captain John Dix, Dixboro was sited along an old Indian trail with well‑drained soils, timber, and a creek for water power. The village plan divided roughly 25 acres into lots plus a public square intended for church and school. It once supported mills and businesses, and local tradition attributes its limited growth to the railroad choosing a Huron River route favoring Ypsilanti.

Title: Washtenaw County War Memorial
Inscription: This memorial honors Washtenaw County soldiers and sailors who served in the U.S. Civil War (1861–1865) and the Spanish‑American War (1898). The inscription states it was erected in 1914 and the monument was later moved from the old courthouse lawn to Forest Hill Cemetery when the present courthouse was built.

Title: The Washtenaw House and Lower Town Decline
Inscription: Using paired 1922 photographs taken after the hotel was condemned, the marker interprets the Washtenaw House’s transformation from a prominent hotel to a rooming house serving “all nationalities and all colors,” and its role in a neighborhood shaped by shared water pumps and everyday street life. The North Side Improvement Association purchased and demolished the deteriorated building in 1927 to improve the Broadway gateway, but redevelopment plans collapsed with the Great Depression; only fragments of nearby historic blocks remain.

Title: The Washtenaw House Hotel
Inscription: Built in 1832 on Broadway by Anson Brown and Edward Fuller, the Washtenaw House was once the largest hotel between Detroit and Chicago, serving stagecoach travelers on routes following older Native American trails. The hotel offered stables, dining and parlors, a bar, a third-floor ballroom, and 32 guest rooms, hosting prominent visitors including Michigan’s first governor Stevens T. Mason. It later changed hands and evolved from a temperance “ardent spirits” policy under William Thompson to a well-known tavern and social venue. Shifting rail and road networks and the University of Michigan’s campus location across the river ultimately redirected city growth away from Lower Town.

Title: Weeping Japanese Cherry (Prunus subhirtella 'Pendula')
Inscription: This tree is presented as a symbol of friendship linked to Japan’s 1912 gift of thousands of cherry trees to the United States. U‑M alumni living in Japan donated this specimen for Dow Field in Nichols Arboretum, where it was planted in 1957. The marker also connects 1957 to the origins of U‑M’s Matthaei Botanical Gardens, established through Regent Frederick C. Matthaei Sr. and Mildred Matthaei’s 200‑acre donation east of Ann Arbor.
Erected by: Matthaei Botanical Gardens and Nichols Arboretum, University of Michigan

Title: Welcome to the Parker Family Mills!
Inscription: The Parker Mill complex interprets water-powered milling and family enterprise along Fleming Creek. William and Mary Parker emigrated from England in 1861; William built a small gristmill in 1873 on earlier sawmill ruins, serving both the family farm and local farmers. A cider building (1887) produced cider and vinegar using waterpower from a dam and millpond. Three generations of Parkers expanded operations into commercial products before apple blight (1914) ended vinegar production; milling gradually declined and ceased in 1959. The marker situates the site within a longer regional history of 19th‑century water-powered industry and settlement.
Erected by: Parker Mill County Park, Washtenaw County Parks and Recreation Commission

Title: West Engineering - West Hall
Inscription: Completed in 1904 (expanded 1910) as the New Engineering Building, this Albert Kahn–associated design anchored the southeast end of the original campus and includes the “Engine Arch.” The building housed the first educational towing tank in the U.S., still used for naval architecture and marine engineering research. It also served as the first home of the College of Architecture (originally within Engineering). Renamed West Engineering after East Engineering opened in 1923, it later became West Hall in 1995 and today houses academic units including the School of Information.
Erected by: History and Traditions Committee, University of Michigan

Title: White Willow (Salix alba)
Inscription: This marker links ecological functions of willows (filtering contaminants and use in wastewater treatment) with the Nichols Arboretum landscape. The White Willow at the Wetland Boardwalk was planted in 1922, and the marker also notes the Peony Garden’s beginnings that same year through a gift from alumnus William E. Upjohn, emphasizing public botanical education and historic plant collections.
Erected by: Matthaei Botanical Gardens and Nichols Arboretum, University of Michigan

Title: William L. Clements Library
Inscription: Regent William L. Clements (served 1910–1933) assembled a major collection of Americana (1492–1800) and donated rare books, newspapers, manuscripts, and maps to the university in 1922, along with funds to build a dedicated library. Designed by Albert Kahn in an Italian Renaissance style and completed in 1923, the Clements Library building supports preservation and scholarly research in American history.
Erected by: History and Traditions Committee, University of Michigan

Title: Wood Shed (archival; permanently removed)
Inscription: This (now-removed) marker described the farm’s Pump House as an early water-supply improvement: a hand-dug well provided water that was hand-pumped and carried for household and animal use until electricity reached the structure in the 1950s. A two-story garage/apartment was attached in the 1950s.

Title: Wood Shed
Inscription: This marker explains household heating logistics before widespread modernization: until the 1950s the house used a wood-burning furnace, with fuel stored in the Wood Shed and carried down exterior basement steps. A day (or more) supply was kept in the basement and replenished as needed, often by children in the family.

Title: Xi Psi Phi Fraternity (Bench Marker)
Inscription: This bench marker commemorates the founding of Xi Psi Phi Dental Fraternity at the University of Michigan on February 8, 1889, and lists its six founders: Lewis C. Thayer, Arthur A. Deyde, Walter H. Booth, William F. Gary, Eldon Waterloo, and Gordon G. McCoy. It was placed on campus for the fraternity’s 50th anniversary and erected in 1939 by Xi Psi Phi.

Title: Native American Land Gift 1817
Inscription: This plaque commemorates the grant of lands from the Ojibwe (Chippewa), Odawa (Ottawa), and Bodewadimi (Potawatomi), through the Treaty of Fort Meigs, which states that “believing they may wish some of their children hereafter educated , [they] do grant to the rector of the the Catholic church of St. Anne of Detroit … and to the corporation of the college at Detroit, for the use of the said college, to be retained or sold, as the said rrector and corporation may judge expedient … ” The rector was Gabriel Richard, a founder and first vice president of the corporation of the college, chartered by the territorial legislature as the University of Michigania in 1817. These lands were eventually sold to the beneift of the University of Michigan, which was relocated to Ann Arbor in 1837.
Erected by: History and Tranditions Committee, University of Michigan
